The fundamental question of causal inference: what would have happened? And how we construct that answer in practice to support critical business decisions.

title: About
text:
I’m a Strategy Consultant and Economist at EY-Parthenon, and a Ph.D. Candidate at ISEG – University of Lisbon, where I also spent three years as a Teaching Assistant in applied econometrics and microeconomics.
My work sits at the intersection of causal inference, forecasting, and strategic decision-making. I design econometric frameworks to answer hard questions: did this policy work? what would have happened without this intervention? how elastic is demand at this price point? I’ve applied these methods across public policy evaluation, financial instrument impact assessment, and pricing strategy — always with the goal of turning rigorous analysis into decisions that actually get made.
This blog is where I think out loud about the methods I use and care about. Some posts work through theory and intuition. Others are hands-on walkthroughs with real code and data. Most are somewhere in between.

What I write about

Counterfactual analysis — synthetic controls, difference-in-differences, interrupted time series
Causal inference — identification strategies, DAGs, potential outcomes framework
Forecasting — probabilistic forecasting, model evaluation, uncertainty quantification
Applied examples — using real and simulated data to answer concrete questions



Background

Ph.D. Candidate in Economics — ISEG, University of Lisbon (2019 – present)
MSc in Applied Econometrics and Forecasting — ISEG, University of Lisbon
BSc in Management — Faculty of Economics, University of Coimbra
Senior Consultant, Strategy & Data Analytics — EY-Parthenon (2024 – present)


All views are my own. Code is available on GitHub.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
text:
Your company launches an aggressive marketing campaign in March. At the end of the month, sales are up 20%. Did the campaign work?
Most corporate data analysts would say yes. That conclusion is incomplete, and in the context of capital allocation it is potentially costly. The correct question is:

What would sales have been in March had the campaign never run?

That hypothetical scenario — the world in which the intervention did not occur — is a counterfactual. Constructing a credible counterfactual is not an academic exercise. It is the core of every causal inference project and the foundation for robust strategic decisions.
The distinction is consequential. A 20% increase in sales might indicate that the campaign was transformative. Alternatively, it might mean sales would have risen regardless, due to seasonality, a competitor’s exit, or organic product virality — in which case the campaign spend would be a pure sunk cost. Without a counterfactual, it is impossible to distinguish correlation from causation.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
text:
Your company launches an aggressive marketing campaign in March. At the end of the month, sales are up 20%. Did the campaign work?

What would sales have been in March had the campaign never run?

That hypothetical scenario — the world in which the intervention did not occur — is a counterfactual. Constructing a credible counterfactual is not an academic exercise. It is the core of every causal inference project and the foundation for robust strategic decisions.
The distinction is consequential. A 20% increase in sales might indicate that the campaign was transformative. title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
text:
The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
The technical constraint is direct: we can never directly observe a counterfactual.
In the real world, each unit of analysis (a customer, a store, a firm, a country) exists in only one state at a time. It either received the treatment, or it did not. We observe one outcome; the other remains permanently hidden.
Holland (1986) formalised this as the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference. In the potential outcomes framework, due originally to Neyman (1923) and later extended by Rubin (1974), the individual causal effect for unit i is defined as:
\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)
where:

Y_i(1) is the outcome if unit i receives treatment,
Y_i(0) is the outcome if unit i does not receive treatment,
\tau_i is the individual causal effect.

In practice, we observe Y_i(1) or Y_i(0), never both simultaneously. The individual causal effect \tau_i is, by construction, unidentifiable for a single unit. This is often referred to in the literature as the missing data problem of causal inference (Rubin, 1974; Imbens and Rubin, 2015).
It is also crucial to consider the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA): a unit’s outcome is unaffected by the treatment assigned to other units, and there is only one version of the treatment. Violations — such as spillovers between customers in a marketing campaign — can invalidate the counterfactual construction and, consequently, the entire causal claim.





The missing counterfactual. For each unit, we observe only one potential outcome. The causal effect (the gap between the two states) is never directly observed.




Because we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit, we are forced to construct a plausible counterfactual from other data. The methodology we choose and the assumptions we invoke define the credibility of the entire causal claim.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: Prediction versus Causal Inference: Pearl’s Ladder
text:
Prediction versus Causal Inference: Pearl’s Ladder
A critical preliminary is the distinction between causal inference and conventional predictive modelling. Conflating the two is the primary source of errors in data-driven strategic decisions.
Prediction asks: given what I observe today, what will happen tomorrow?
Causal inference asks: if I intervene and alter variable X, what happens as a direct consequence of that alteration?
A machine learning model can be outstanding for prediction and entirely uninformative for strategic decision-making. The classical illustration: umbrella sales predict rainfall with high accuracy. Subsidising umbrella production does not cause rain. If you are a public official deciding on drainage infrastructure investment, knowing that “umbrella sales are elevated” is a useful forecast but carries zero causal content.
Pearl (2009) formalised this distinction with the do-calculus. Observational data provides P(Y \mid X = x), the distribution of outcomes conditional on observing X = x. Causal inference, by contrast, targets P(Y \mid do(X = x)), the distribution of outcomes if we were to force X = x through an external intervention. These are distinct mathematical objects. The do-operator represents a graph surgery on the causal model: it severs all edges into X and fixes its value, removing any confounding by variables that previously determined X.
This distinction maps onto Pearl’s three-rung hierarchy of causation (Pearl and Mackenzie, 2018): (1) association, which captures statistical correlations and is sufficient for prediction; (2) intervention, which asks what happens if we act, corresponding to do-expressions; and (3) counterfactuals, which ask what would have happened to a specific unit under conditions contrary to those actually observed. Standard regression and machine learning operate exclusively at the first rung. Causal inference targets the second and third.
To make causal assumptions explicit and visualise the direction of causal relationships, analysts use Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs). A DAG helps identify potential confounders and the correct adjustment set. For example, in the marketing scenario, a DAG reveals how seasonality (Z) can influence both the decision to run a campaign (X) and sales (Y) simultaneously, making it a confounder that must be controlled for valid causal inference:





graph LR
    Z[Seasonality] --> X(Marketing Campaign)
    Z --> Y(Sales)
    X --> Y

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: The Identification Landscape: How We Build Counterfactuals
text:
The Identification Landscape: How We Build Counterfactuals
Since the counterfactual is not directly observable, every causal identification strategy is, in practice, an answer to a single question: which other data can validly substitute for the missing potential outcome?
The following table summarises the principal strategies, grouped by the structure of data they exploit, together with their identifying assumptions, key vulnerabilities, and typical business applications.






NotePrincipal identification strategies













Strategy
Core assumption
Counterfactual comes from
Key vulnerability
Business context




Randomised experiment (RCT)
Random assignment of treatment
The control group, by construction
External validity (generalisation to real-world conditions)
A/B tests, product optimisation, campaign evaluation


Difference-in-Differences (DiD)
Parallel trends absent treatment
A control group with a parallel time trend
Violation of the parallel trends assumption; TWFE bias under staggered adoption
Policy evaluation (e.g., price changes), regulatory impact


Synthetic control
A weighted combination of donors fits pre-treatment trends
An artificial composite from a donor pool
Interpolation bias; arbitrary pool selection
Impact of singular events (e.g., mergers, new laws) on specific markets


Regression Discontinuity (RD)
Units just above and below a threshold are comparable
Units on the other side of the cutoff
Strictly local validity (estimates hold only near the cutoff)
Programmes with eligibility thresholds (e.g., subsidies, promotions)


Instrumental Variables (IV)
Instrument shifts treatment but does not affect the outcome directly
Exogenous variation induced by the instrument
Weak instruments; violation of the exclusion restriction
Endogenous interventions (e.g., education, health programmes)


G-computation / G-formula
No unmeasured confounders (conditional exchangeability)
Predicted outcomes from a correctly specified outcome model
Model misspecification; sensitivity to sparse data
Complex or sequential treatments; rich covariate settings





Each strategy requires a different identifying assumption. The quality of a causal claim is only as strong as the plausibility of that assumption in the specific empirical context. For a practitioner, the choice of strategy is not merely technical but strategic: it depends on data availability and on which assumptions can be credibly defended.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: Building Counterfactuals in Practice: The ATT
text:
Building Counterfactuals in Practice: The ATT
Consider a common operational scenario: a unit is observed over time, an intervention occurs at a known point, and we want to estimate the intervention’s effect. The counterfactual is the trajectory the unit would have followed in the absence of the intervention.





Counterfactual trajectory. We observe the treated unit up to the point of intervention. In the post-period, two paths diverge: what was actually observed, and what would have occurred absent the treatment.




The Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) is the average gap between the observed and counterfactual trajectories across the post-treatment period. At time t, it is defined as:
ATT(t) = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) \mid D_i = 1]
The estimation problem is that we observe only one of the two lines. Every identification strategy is a different method for recovering the red dashed line.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
text:
Counterfactuals by Data Structure
The appropriate strategy depends intrinsically on the structure of the available data. The three principal settings are time-series data, panel data, and cross-sectional data with covariates.

Time-series counterfactuals
When a single unit is observed over time (a country, a firm, a city) and an intervention occurs at a known date, the counterfactual is the projection of what the series would have done absent the intervention.
The standard tool is to fit a model to the pre-intervention period and extrapolate it into the post-period. This is the logic underlying CausalImpact (Brodersen et al., 2015), which models the pre-treatment period with a Bayesian structural time-series model using auxiliary covariates as controls. It is also the logic of Causal ARIMA (Menchetti, Cipollini, and Mealli, 2023), which extends ARIMA models to a causal inference setting, and of synthetic control methods applied to a single treated unit.
The credibility of this approach depends entirely on two conditions: the quality of the model’s fit during the pre-intervention period, and the absence of other concurrent events (exogenous shocks) that coincide with the intervention. A good in-sample fit does not guarantee a valid out-of-sample extrapolation when there is an unmodelled structural break.





Time-series counterfactual via ARIMA extrapolation. A model is fitted on the pre-treatment data and extrapolated into the post-treatment period. The gap between the observed series and the forecast is the estimated causal effect.






Panel data counterfactuals: Difference-in-Differences
When multiple units are observed over time and only a subset receives treatment, the untreated units can serve as the counterfactual for the treated units. This is the foundation of Difference-in-Differences (DiD).
The key identifying assumption is parallel trends: in the absence of treatment, the treated and control groups would have followed the same temporal trajectory. Under this assumption, the counterfactual for the treated group after treatment is their pre-treatment level plus the trend observed in the control group.





Difference-in-Differences. Under the parallel trends assumption, the evolution of the control group after treatment begins informs what the treated group would have done absent the intervention.




The DiD estimator is:
\widehat{ATT} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{\text{treated, post}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{treated, pre}})}_{\text{change in treated}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{\text{control, post}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{control, pre}})}_{\text{change in control}}
The control group’s trend is subtracted from the treated group’s change. The residual — the portion of the change not attributable to a common trend — is the estimated causal effect.
An event study plot is the standard visual tool for interrogating the parallel trends assumption. Pre-treatment coefficients should be statistically indistinguishable from zero; divergence begins only at the treatment date.





Event study for Difference-in-Differences. The parallel trends assumption is visually assessed in the pre-treatment period. Treatment effects emerge as the two group trajectories diverge post-treatment.




A note on recent methodological developments: the DiD literature has undergone substantial revision since Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) and Goodman-Bacon (2021) demonstrated that the canonical Two-Way Fixed Effects (TWFE) estimator can produce severely biased estimates under staggered treatment adoption when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over time. In such settings, already-treated units can act as “forbidden comparisons” in the TWFE decomposition. Robust alternatives — including the Callaway–Sant’Anna group-time average treatment effect estimator and the stacked regression approach (Baker et al., 2022) — are now standard practice for staggered DiD designs.


Cross-sectional counterfactuals: G-computation
When cross-sectional data with covariates are available but no natural control group or time series exists, counterfactuals can still be constructed through G-computation, also known as the g-formula or outcome regression standardisation (Robins, 1986; Hernán and Robins, 2020).
The procedure follows three steps. First, fit an outcome model \hat{E}[Y \mid X, D] using the observed data. Second, use this fitted model to impute each unit’s outcome under a hypothetical intervention — setting D = 1 for all units (to obtain \hat{Y}_i(1)) and D = 0 for all units (to obtain \hat{Y}_i(0)), while holding covariates X_i at their observed values. Third, compare the two distributions of imputed outcomes.
Under conditional exchangeability — treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes conditional on the observed covariates, D \perp\!\!\!\perp \{Y(0), Y(1)\} \mid X — this procedure yields a consistent estimate of the causal effect (Robins, 1986).





G-computation: each unit’s outcome is predicted under D = 1 and D = 0 by setting the treatment indicator while holding covariates at their observed values. The ATE is the average of unit-level differences.




The Average Treatment Effect (ATE) under g-computation is:
\widehat{ATE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \hat{E}[Y \mid X_i, D=1] - \hat{E}[Y \mid X_i, D=0] \right]
This approach is notably flexible: it accommodates any outcome model, handles both continuous and discrete treatments, and naturally extends to the analysis of heterogeneous treatment effects by examining the distribution of unit-level effects rather than only the mean.
The principal vulnerability is model misspecification. If the outcome model is incorrectly specified, the counterfactual predictions inherit that misspecification. This is the rationale for doubly robust estimators such as the Augmented Inverse Probability Weighting (AIPW) estimator (Robins, Rotnitzky, and Zhao, 1994), which combines outcome regression with propensity score weighting. If either model is correctly specified, the estimate remains consistent. Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE), developed by van der Laan and Rubin (2006), provides a semiparametric, doubly robust alternative that also permits flexible machine learning nuisance models while preserving valid statistical inference.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: Three Estimands: ATE, ATT, and ATU
text:
Three Estimands: ATE, ATT, and ATU
A recurring source of confusion in applied work is the conflation of distinct causal estimands. G-computation makes the distinction transparent because the estimand is directly determined by the population over which we standardise.
The Average Treatment Effect (ATE) is the expected causal effect if a randomly selected unit from the full population were treated:
ATE = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)]
The Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) conditions on the treated subpopulation:
ATT = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) \mid D_i = 1]
The Average Treatment Effect on the Untreated (ATU) conditions on the control subpopulation:
ATU = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) \mid D_i = 0]
The ATE, ATT, and ATU coincide when treatment effect heterogeneity is absent or when treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes (as in a randomised experiment). In observational settings with selection into treatment, the three can differ substantially. A job training programme, for instance, may exhibit a large ATT because the population that self-selects into training differs systematically from those who do not, and the programme may be less effective for the latter group.
The practical implication is that the choice of estimand should be determined by the decision question, not by computational convenience (Hernán and Robins, 2020; Arel-Bundock et al., 2024).

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
section: Causal Inference as Competitive Advantage: ROI and Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Causal Inference as Competitive Advantage: ROI and Sensitivity Analysis
For strategists and consultants, causal inference is not merely a matter of statistical rigour — it is a tool for optimising Return on Investment and mitigating risk. Confusing correlation with causation carries a real cost: misallocated capital and suboptimal decisions.
Rather than simply observing that a campaign lifted sales, causal inference allows you to quantify the net increment attributable to the intervention. This transforms the analysis from a descriptive report into a portfolio optimisation tool. Estimating the ATT allows you to answer: “What would be the sales loss if we discontinued the campaign?” or “What is the marginal gain from extending it?”
Crucially, all causal identification strategies rest on assumptions. The credibility of a causal claim is directly proportional to the plausibility of those assumptions. In a consulting context, it is imperative to go beyond point estimates and conduct a sensitivity analysis — testing how robust results are to small violations of key assumptions (e.g., parallel trends, no unmeasured confounders). Approaches include placebo tests in DiD designs, and E-value analysis to quantify how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain away the observed effect. Presenting a confidence interval alongside a sensitivity analysis substantially increases both the credibility and the practical utility of strategic recommendations.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
text:
Connecting the Approaches
The three strategies described above operate on the same underlying logic at different levels of data abstraction:

What would have happened to this unit had things been different?
We construct the answer using different sources of variation — time-series trends, cross-unit comparisons, or covariate-adjusted predictions.
The causal claim is only as credible as the assumption that our constructed counterfactual is a valid stand-in for the unobserved one.






Three data structures, three counterfactual strategies.

title: What Is a Counterfactual? The Key to Causality-Based Strategic Decisions
text:
What Comes Next
This post has introduced the foundational concepts. Subsequent posts will go deeper on each identification strategy:

Difference-in-Differences: from the canonical 2×2 case to staggered adoption and the pitfalls of TWFE
Synthetic control: building an artificial counterfactual from a donor pool
G-computation and doubly robust estimators: when rich covariate data are available but no natural experiment exists
Regression Discontinuity: exploiting threshold rules for local identification
Event studies: testing parallel trends and tracing treatment effect dynamics over time
